summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the OAuth redirect validation in FeathersJS can be bypassed using URL-encoded payloads. The regex validation at strategy.ts:111 only checks for literal '@', '//', and '\' characters but fails to validate URL-encoded versions like '%40', '%2F%2F', and '%5C'. These encoded payloads pass validation but are decoded by the browser, allowing attackers to redirect OAuth callbacks to external domains and capture authentication tokens.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate that URL-encoded redirect payloads bypass the validation regex and would allow attackers to redirect OAuth authentication flows to attacker-controlled domains, enabling token theft and account takeover.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction script successfully identified multiple bypass payloads including '%40attacker.com', '%2F%2Fattacker.com', '%5Cattacker.com', and '%2540attacker.com'. These payloads pass the vulnerable regex validation at packages/authentication-oauth/src/strategy.ts:111 because the pattern /[@\\]|^\/\/|\/\// only matches literal characters, not their URL-encoded equivalents. When concatenated to the allowed origin and decoded by the browser, these payloads create URLs like 'https://target.com@attacker.com' which redirect to the attacker's domain.
verification_method: The test_redirect_bypass.py script systematically tests the validation regex with various payloads and identifies which URL-encoded variants bypass the protection. It then demonstrates how the browser would interpret the resulting URLs, showing that 'https://target.com%40attacker.com' is decoded and redirected to 'attacker.com' rather than 'target.com'. The verification distinguishes between payloads that are blocked (literal '@', '//', '\') and those that bypass the validation via encoding.
evidence_produced_by_run: The regex pattern /[@\\]|^\/\/|\/\// was tested against 7 different payload variations
Direct payloads ('@attacker.com', '//attacker.com', '\\attacker.com') are correctly blocked by the validation
URL-encoded payloads ('%40attacker.com', '%2F%2Fattacker.com', '%5Cattacker.com', '%2540attacker.com') all bypass the validation
Browser URL parsing demonstrates that 'https://target.com%40attacker.com' would be interpreted as 'https://target.com@attacker.com', redirecting to attacker.com
The vulnerable code at strategy.ts:115 concatenates the allowed origin with the user-provided redirect parameter without URL decoding, allowing the encoded payload to reach the browser
Session storage at service.ts:177 persists the malicious redirect parameter without sanitization, enabling the attack across the OAuth flow

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit v5.0.41, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
